title: Typical and Atypical Intersections: Geometry, Dynamics, and Applications
speaker: Gregorio Baldi
abstract:
Many geometric spaces carry natural collections of special submanifolds that encode their internal symmetries. Examples include abelian varieties and their sub-abelian varieties, locally symmetric spaces with their totally geodesic subspaces, period domains with their sub–period domains, and strata of abelian differentials with their affine invariant submanifolds.
In recent years, major progress has been made in understanding these structures through the framework of unlikely intersections and functional transcendence. In the first lecture, I will survey how this perspective can be applied to the study of non-arithmetic complex hyperbolic lattices and affine invariant submanifolds, complementing existing dynamical approaches. In the second lecture, I will outline the proof of the geometric Zilber–Pink conjecture and discuss general results on the distribution of the Hodge locus.

proof_stages.label: Homogeneous arithmeticity framework
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up locally symmetric manifolds S=X_G/Gamma, defines lattices and arithmetic lattices, and poses the problem of recognizing arithmeticity from geometry.

proof_stages.label: Arithmeticity criteria and totally geodesic consequences
proof_stages.summary: Margulis-type arithmeticity and the commensurator criterion are recalled, and the algebraic condition of a large commensurator is translated into the geometric production of many totally geodesic submanifolds.

proof_stages.label: McMullen-type converse finiteness theorem
proof_stages.summary: A partial converse is stated: in the relevant real or complex hyperbolic settings, infinitely many maximal totally geodesic submanifolds of dimension greater than one force arithmeticity, so nonarithmetic quotients have only finitely many such submanifolds.

proof_stages.label: Translation-surface orbit-closure setting
proof_stages.summary: The nonhomogeneous analogue is introduced via strata of abelian differentials, GL_2(R)-orbit closures, their rank and field-of-definition degree, and the linearity technology of Eskin-Mirzakhani-Mohammadi/Wright-type results.

proof_stages.label: Teichmüller-geodesic reformulation
proof_stages.summary: The orbit-closure finiteness problem is reformulated in M_{g,n} as a finiteness theorem for totally geodesic subvarieties for the Teichmüller/Kobayashi metric, with lifts to strata relating the two formulations.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic approximation as an ambient intersection space
proof_stages.summary: For a complex hyperbolic quotient, the adjoint trace field and associated k-form give an arithmetic approximation A(S); a totally geodesic submanifold Y has its own approximation A(Y), and morally Y is realized as S intersected with A(Y) inside A(S), with nonarithmeticity making the intersection atypical.

proof_stages.label: Hodge-theoretic realization and Zilber-Pink finiteness
proof_stages.summary: Local rigidity and H^1-vanishing for the adjoint local system are used to package the Galois conjugates into a Q/Z-variation of Hodge structure and a period map, after which geometric Zilber-Pink gives finiteness of maximal atypical intersections and hence of maximal totally geodesic submanifolds.

proof_stages.label: Parallel atypical-intersection strategy for translation surfaces
proof_stages.summary: In the moduli-of-curves setting, the same philosophy realizes orbit closures as intersections in a Hodge bundle over a mixed Shimura-type base with an additional one-form direction, where the rank and degree hypotheses force atypicality beyond standard mixed Zilber-Pink.

examples.name: Standard symmetric-space models
examples.note: Real hyperbolic space, complex hyperbolic space, and Siegel space are offered as concrete models for the locally symmetric quotients being discussed.
examples.engagement_reason: the standard symmetric spaces are listed as models to keep in mind, with associated groups named but no concrete calculation on them

examples.name: Classical arithmetic lattices
examples.note: Groups such as SL_2(Z), Sp_{2g}(Z), and SL_n(Z) illustrate the notion that integral points in a real Lie group give finite-volume quotients.
examples.engagement_reason: classical arithmetic lattices such as integral-point groups are cited as examples and said to have finite volume without working through a specific case

examples.name: Riemann-surface lattices in SL_2(R)
examples.note: The continuum of hyperbolic Riemann surfaces is used to explain why SL_2(R) is exceptional: there are far more lattices than the countably many arithmetic ones.
examples.engagement_reason: Riemann-surface lattices in SL_2(R) are invoked to explain that there are continuously many deformations, but no particular lattice is analyzed

examples.name: Ball quotient surface with quadratic trace field
examples.note: The case n=2, m=1 and trace-field degree two is worked through to show that a one-dimensional totally geodesic intersection has larger-than-expected dimension, revealing the atypical-intersection mechanism.
examples.engagement_reason: the ball quotient surface with degree-two trace field is used in a concrete dimension count showing an actual one-dimensional intersection where the expected dimension is zero

passes.reason: the n=2 quadratic-trace-field ball quotient is a minimal locally symmetric case used to display an excess-dimensional atypical intersection.
The quadratic trace-field ball quotient is a minimal nontrivial trace-field case, and it is used to show the expected zero-dimensional intersection fails by producing a curve.
A ball quotient surface is locally symmetric and the quadratic trace field is the minimal non-rational trace-field case used to illustrate atypical intersection.
A locally symmetric complex-hyperbolic surface in the minimal nontrivial quadratic-trace-field case is used to exhibit an excess-dimensional intersection.
A ball quotient surface is locally symmetric, and the quadratic trace-field case is used to exhibit an excess-dimensional intersection over the expected dimension.
The complex-hyperbolic surface with quadratic trace field is presented as the first/minimal nontrivial dimension-degree case for the atypical-intersection heuristic.
The ball quotient surface with quadratic trace field is the minimal nontrivial complex-hyperbolic surface case in the dimension-count example and is locally symmetric.
It is used to exhibit an excess-dimensional atypical intersection, and the surface with quadratic trace field is the minimal nontrivial ball-quotient dimension-count case.
The quadratic-trace-field ball quotient is the minimal non-arithmetic toy case realizing an excess-dimensional atypical intersection.

A ball quotient surface is locally complex-hyperbolic and attains the ball-quotient Miyaoka–Yau equality, but here it illustrates the atypical-intersection dimension count rather than refuting a claim.

The quadratic trace field gives the minimal non-rational trace-field degree in the ball-quotient dimension-count example, used to illustrate atypicality rather than refute a claim.
